data.title: Lynas Rare Earths: Long-Term Price Floor Is Transformational But Seems To Be Partially Priced In (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Intro

Lynas (LYSCF) stock has rallied more than 40% since my last report, which included a BUY rating back in September 2025. Based on recent results, I do adjust my DCF upward a bit, but the current share price seems to reflect the fundamentals. Any further upside could come from an NdPr price above $115/kg or from information that would help us value the upstream magnet option, but neither is happening at the moment. As a result, I believe it is time to take some profits and am changing my rating from BUY to HOLD.

Investment overview

Before going into Lynas’s financials, let’s have a brief look at the latest developments in the rare earth market.

The rare earth market has been at the center of geopolitical tensions, serving as a tool for China to counter US commercial hostilities through tariffs and tech restrictions. As shown below, Chinese high-power magnet exports collapsed when tensions with the US were highest, in May 2025. It is worth noting that China dominates the Rare Earth market with a close to 70% share and has a quasi-total monopoly in magnets, with Japan as the only material player in the field.

In July 2025, the US Department of Defense invested in MP Materials (MP), with the acquisition of $400m worth of preferred stock convertible into shares of the Company’s common stock. In addition, the DoD will hold a warrant permitting to purchase additional shares.

The US DoD also set a $110/kg price floor for a defined volume. This measure began pushing Chinese prices upward, as shown in the following picture. In March 2025, Lynas struck a major deal with its Japanese partner in the Jare JV. The agreed terms show the counterpart will commit to buying between 5kt and 7.2tk of NdPr metal, with a price floor of $110/kg and profit sharing above $150/kg. An existing deal allowed the purchase of up to 75% of Lynas’s heavy rare-earth metals. These events occurred in a context in which China materially limited rare-earth exports to Japan, beginning in early 2026.

Let’s now turn on Lynas most recent results, S1FY26.

Malaysian production site is doing well, with first Heavy Rare Eaths Oxydes processed in the Q4 2025 while Samarium production target is set at the Q4 FY26.

However, electric grid instabilities caused operational issues at the Kalgoorlie Australian plant, negatively impacting NdPr production, as shown in the following picture. The management is focused on implementing off-grid solutions for its sites. A prime example comes from the Mont Weld site, where a large energy plant composed of a 7W solar farm, 24MW wind turbines, 12MW battery storage, and a Gas plant is providing reliable energy at a reasonable cost.

The Mt Weld expansion project was largely commissioned, with the new flotation circuit operating at 70% of nameplate capacity. During the conference call, the CEO insisted that the roadmap toward 10.5kt of NdPr production is fully on track and could be achieved by CY2027, equivalent to FY2028.

Over the last few months, the firm has raised AUD930m through equity issuance, a bit more that management's expectations (AUD902m) outlined in the 2030 strategy release. A portion of this amount will be used to develop and commercialize NdFeB permanent magnet manufacturing capacity.

Risks and Opportunities

Lynas and other rare-earth stocks can be quite volatile, as shown below. While underlying demand market trends are clear : auto and EV growth, robotics, electronics, and defense, the supply side is more difficult to handle. As a result, the sector can attract significant speculation, leading to stock prices diverging from their intrinsic values. For instance, shares of MP Materials (MP) have fallen sharply from their highs despite the strength of the business case and solid deals with the US DoD. Fortunately, Lynas is already on the way to generating healthy, measurable cash flows, as we will see in the DCF model, so it is less prone to share price volatility.

A material opportunity could come from the upstream development. Lynas now has sufficient liquidity to develop a fully integrated ex-China supply chain from REE oxides to NdPr metal and magnets. While difficult to model, such an option must have a high value.

What valuation can we expect?

I used the central hypothesis:

  • Lynas projected volume expansion, toward 12kt per year in the mid-term.
  • I model NdPr prices close to $110/kg for FY2026 as a deal has been struck with the Jare JV, and market prices already seem to outpace this level. Going forward, I anticipate a gradual appreciation, a bit above the world’s inflation rate.
  • Growing S&A to support upstream expansion.
  • CAPEX remains quite elevated at AUD200m per year, for a couple of years, to support magnet production.

To provide more perspective, I conducted a sensitivity analysis varying the NdPr price.

We see that the current share price discounts NdPr prices close to $115/kg. More tensions in the rare-earth market, or an acceleration in underlying markets, would be needed to justify further upside in the shares. At this stage, it is difficult to model the extent to which Lynas will gain from its future magnet production, which can be viewed as a free option in the business case.

Conclusion

Lynas has dethroned MP Material in terms of operational metrics and stock performance. The 2030 plan seems well-founded and well-supported by a strong balance sheet. The firm is on the way to fully integrate from mining to magnet production. With a guaranteed NdPr price floor, the stock has had a major run over recent weeks, limiting upside potential. With a lot of good news already priced in, I believe it is time to take profits on the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Greggs: Macro Outlook Worsens, But Shares Remain Cheap
data.description:
It's easy to see why bakery chain Greggs plc (OTCPK:GGGSF)(OTCPK:GGGSY) is one of the most shorted companies on the London Stock Exchange. As one of the U.K.'s leading quick-service restaurant firms, the company has been particularly sensitive to weak consumer sentiment and cost inflation, the financial impact of which has been exacerbated by a period of elevated investment spending. And now, we can add the war in Iran to the list of its problems.

I last covered Greggs in October. Upgrading it to "Strong Buy", I primarily liked the valuation, as Greggs' shares had slumped to a trailing P/E ratio of just 12, well below the 20x multiple they had typically enjoyed in "normal" times. While that reflected a difficult operating environment, investors could take some comfort from the fact Greggs' woes were largely not brand-specific, evidenced by the fact that the company wasn't losing market share. So, it was fair to assume that Greggs (and its stock) would recover alongside any broader macro recovery.

The conflict in the Middle East clearly presents an untimely headwind for the firm. That said, there are some bright spots to cling to, and with the shares trading on a similar valuation compared to my last update, I remain bullish.

An Untimely Headwind

To quickly recap, Greggs' estate consists of around 2,750 stores in the United Kingdom. While Greggs does have a growing franchised business (just over 600 shops), most of its outlets are operated by the company, meaning it is sensitive to the headwinds facing store-level economics.

Last year, Greggs booked a total trading profit of £317.9 million. £251.4 million came from its company-managed shops, 9% lower than 2024. Fragile consumer health has been one problem. The obvious indicator for this is like-for-like sales growth, which was 2.4% last year across its company-operated estate. Excluding COVID-affected 2020, that was the weakest print in at least a decade.

Revenue growth in this part of the business was better, up 6.5%, as the number of company-managed stores increased by 4%. However, this brings me to the other headwind: cost inflation. Last year, expenses associated with company-managed stores rose at a faster pace than sales. Digging a little deeper, labor was an 8% headwind due to a rise in the minimum wage and employers' National Insurance (basically a tax on payroll). Food & packaging inflation was also stubborn in the mid-single-digit area. Together, these two lines account for over 70% of the underlying cost base.

With the above in mind, the war in the Middle East is an untimely headwind. On the demand side, U.K. households are looking at a squeeze in their incomes, with costs set to rise in a number of areas.

On the subject of costs, Greggs has actually hedged 100% of its electricity expenses for 2026, which is great given how reliant the U.K. is on natural gas, the price of which has risen sharply (as shown above). The company has also fixed prices on four months' worth of food and packaging. While these moves are obviously welcome and will shield the company to some extent, we're still looking at negative impact overall.

Bright Spots

I referenced some bright spots in the introduction. One is the fact that Greggs' issues are really macro-driven rather than anything company-specific. In fact, Greggs actually increased its share of total U.K. food-to-go visits by 50 basis points last year, as shown below. When the market recovers, Greggs should as well.

Another positive is the winding down of its investment program. To recap, Greggs' CapEx increased by around 160% between 2022 and 2025. This was largely due to investments in new logistics and manufacturing centers, which the firm needs in order to supply its growing store count.

2025 was the peak of this investment cycle. Management expects capital spending to fall around £90 million this year, before dropping by another £30-£50 million in 2027. Regardless of how the wider macro environment impacts the business, normalizing CapEx will be a significant tailwind to free cash flow.

The strength of Greggs' balance sheet should also offer some comfort. The company only had £25 million in outstanding borrowings at the end of last year against roughly £70 million in cash.

Finally, the non-company-managed part of Greggs' business has been doing better, with profits rising 20% last year to £66.5 million. This includes the franchised part of the estate, where the company makes money from licensee fees and through the sale of supplies to stores.

Greggs had 602 franchised stores at the end of 2025, up 7% year-on-year. There is good reason to think this number will head higher over time. That's because store-level profitability is very healthy. We can see this in the data from company-managed stores. Per the last section, these shops made £251.4 million in profits last year. Given an average of roughly 2,100 company-managed shops in the estate, that would imply a profit per unit of £120,000. Above, you can see that Greggs has disclosed an average cost per shop of £430,000. This would imply an attractive cash-on-cash return of 25%-30%, which is a strong incentive to open up new stores.

The Valuation

Greggs' valuation was central to my investment case, and that hasn't changed. The shares trade for £15.51 as of this writing (equal to around $6.84 per ADS), mapping to a multiple of just 12.6x last year's earnings per share (£1.228). Historically, Greggs typically traded for around 20x earnings (earnings were negative in 2020 due to COVID, which is why that year is blank in the chart below).

Greggs also pays a dividend. It was £0.69 per share last year, for a trailing yield of 4.4%. This also remains attractive compared to historical levels.

As I mentioned, this discount isn't being driven by debt. Greggs is in a net cash position. It simply reflects the weak economic outlook, and remember, Greggs is actually outperforming the wider U.K. takeaway market right now. The company continues to grow it store count as well. At some point, macro conditions will stop being a headwind, and these factors will translate into higher profits for Greggs. Even if the stock only gets back to a P/E multiple of 15-16, this would be an extra 20-25% tailwind on top of its earnings growth. Tack on the 4.4% dividend yield, and today's buyer has a good chance of generating attractive returns, though it may take several years for this to play out. Accordingly, I am leaving my "Strong Buy" rating in place.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Strategic Education: Should Keep Shareholders Well-Fed In 2026
data.description:
Everyone (and probably their dogs and mothers) should know by now that for-profit education stocks are tough.

Still, if you dig deep enough, you'll eventually find gold at the right price.

That’s how I see Strategic Education (STRA).

Some folks and I here at Seeking Alpha have been shouting for some time that this stock is dirt-cheap at ~6x EBITDA.

Of course, Higher-Ed enrollment is under pressure in the U.S. and NZ/Australia. You see this from the 1.5% drop in consolidated enrollments in FY 2025 (-1.4% at Strayer/Capella and -1.8% at Torrens).

But here's the catch. Not everything is about enrollment.

  1. Strategic is using (very well, by the way) AI to cut expenses. In FY 2025 alone, they cut approximately $30 million (just over 7% of SG&A) and invested in high-growth areas. In Q4 FY 2025 alone, they increased the EBIT margin of Strayer/Capella and Torrens by 470 and 270 bp, respectively.
  2. The ETS segment (primarily Sophia Learning) is growing much faster than Higher-Ed (41.4% in FY 2025 alone) and now represents almost 12% of the total. So this is pushing up Strategic's EBIT margin, with ETS running at ~39.6% against 15.5% consolidated.
  3. Workforce Edge is the perfect win-win acquisition funnel for Strategic. Besides reducing CAC, this also makes them eligible for OBBB tailwinds (primarily the Workforce Pell Grant). It's the classic tailwind for for-profits focused on the job market with highly employable courses, something similar to Grand Canyon (LOPE).

For me, the mistake is treating Strategic like just another slow, counter-cyclical for-profit.

Strategic Tomorrow: Modeling FY 2026

Now the fun part.

Management didn't give me much to work with, so I'll have to fill in a few gaps.

I don't need to use much of my creativity here. It’s quite clear to me that ETS will continue to grow (even with EBIT having peaked) and U.S. Higher-Ed will have some more lift in margins.

The only drag seems to be Torrens.

Even though international student commencements in Australia were up roughly 25,000, restrictions are still tight. I still think they won't be able to compensate for that with domestic demand.

I'm going to set up three different scenarios to model here:

  • Base Scenario: Revenue continues to grow at a mid-single-digit rate (~$1.33 billion). This is the Higher-Ed flat rate while ETS continues at the same pace. For EBIT, I imagine the margin will stretch a little further to between 16 and 16.5% ($212.8 to $219.4 million).
  • Bull Scenario: Here I imagine revenue increasing to a high-single-digit, reaching something close to $1.38 billion. For this to happen, Higher-Ed and Torrens probably need to grow at a low single digit rate with ETS at the same pace as FY 2025. The EBIT margin could reach ~17% (EBIT of $234.6 million).
  • Bear Scenario: For me, the worst-case scenario is revenue remaining flat year-over-year at $1.27 billion and the EBIT margin also remaining flat ($197 million EBIT). I don't see this slipping due to both cost cuts and improvements in the mix.

If I also model an EBIT-to-OCF conversion between 100% and 110% (a range I've been fluctuating over the last two years), Strategic would generate an OCF between $216.1 million and $237.7 million using the midpoint of my projected EBIT.

Since they've kept Capex close to $40 million in recent years, I can also pinpoint something close to that, and we'd have ~$187 million in FCF on the table.

Another interesting take here is share buybacks and dividends.

Well, currently they're sitting on an authorization of $200 million. If they return ~$90 million in FY 2026, and from the FCF you can see that it's possible, the buyback yield would be ~4.6%.

They'll probably return more than that by issuing some more authorization along the way.

I'll aim for a mid-to-high single digit then.

Regarding dividends, they've never been known for giving much of a lift.

Even assuming the $0.60 will be paid normally, the yield would be 2.9%. That's roughly $13.6 million returned here.

Adding it all up, I'm expecting a high single-digit shareholder yield. And the best part is that doing this would return just over $103 million, leaving a nice chunk for them to sit on.

I think it's clear that they sustain this shareholder yield in all scenarios, isn't it?

Why This Stock Is Still Cheap

If I simply take the 5-year average EV/EBITDA multiple of ~15x from Strategic here, I'd arrive at a price target of $142.55. A huge upside.

But I don't want to be naive.

People are probably paying structurally lower multiples for for-profit education at this point.

So, if I take peers like Covista (CVSA), Perdoceo (PRDO), Laureate (LAUR), and American Public Education (APEI), the average multiple is 11.1x. This gives me a price target of ~$106.

Slightly above my previous range, but still credible for FY 2026.

Even if you look at the bear scenario (~$99 per share), there's double-digit upside on the table.

But what if we try to look at ETS separately? I mean, this fast-growing segment probably deserves higher multiples than the traditional Higher-Ed business.

I think we all agree on that here. And this is where the hidden value could appear.

For example, if I imagine another 30-40% growth in ETS revenue, that could reach approximately $200 million. If I assume the rest remains flat (a little over $1.1 billion), I already have what I need for a SOTP.

For ETS, I imagine they deserve higher multiples than Coursera (COUR) or even Udemy (UDMY) given their relative profitability. Of course, I don't envision a Duolingo-like (DUOL), but something close to 2.5x sales.

In the traditional business model, I'll use the average of the asset-heavy peers I mentioned before plus some haircut. That would give me something close to 2x. Adding everything up and including net cash, we could find something close to $120 per share.

That's quite bold.

The market would need to evaluate ETS on its own, and probably wouldn't do so anytime soon.

To triangulate this, I'll take a look at FY 2028.

I don't know if you know, but three years ago management gave us very clear targets for FY 2028: revenue of $1.5 billion and an EBIT margin of ~20% ($300 million).

So, if I plug that into my model with that EV/EBIT of 11.1x and discount it to present value at a cost of equity of ~7.5%, I also find those ~$120.

I'm talking about an upside of just over 27% in the short term (up to $106, and if you add the shareholder yield, it easily exceeds 30% total return) and almost 45% in the medium term.

It's a setup that reiterates the 'Strong Buy' in my opinion.

This is a thesis that doesn't depend solely on multiple expansion. Shareholder could sit back and enjoy the fat distributions along the way while waiting for the market to re-rate ETS.

Risks on the Table

While I believe the 'Strong Buy' is well protected at these levels (as you can see in the upside of the bear scenario), I still see some risks out there.

And of course, the first of these is the secular decline of Higher-Ed.

As I told you before, to a certain extent there was cyclical growth here. For example, during the GFC and for a couple of years after that, enrollments exploded as people had more time to return to college.

Nowadays, that effect probably wouldn't move the needle as much. With some exceptions, I think. Nursing (healthcare courses in general) and technical courses still have strong enrollment due to higher job market demand.

AI might cut some costs, but at the end of the day I imagine that if enrollments continue to fall they will have to migrate to something more like asset-light.

That day is still far away. But it's worth thinking about scenarios like this.

Torrens is another drag.

Without international students, I don't see enrollment returning to pre-pandemic levels.

I told you in the last article that the average Australian didn't support the old immigration policies, and they will probably be tightened (perhaps in this or the next administration). Another blow to enrollments, probably.

Not explosive, but something to continue declining in the low to mid-single digits annually.

data.title: Energy Fuels: From Hold To Buy As The Story Changes
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It has been some time since I wrote about Energy Fuels (UUUU) in my previous article. My opinion was to hold. Why? Because at that time, I found the story to be quite compelling. The issue was that I did not feel there had been enough confirmation to support the level of optimism I felt there was regarding the future of the company. In addition, I believed the valuation reflected confidence in advance. Presently, I believe we are seeing evidence of changes that will likely cause me to modify my thesis.

What am I referring to? Simply put, I believe Energy Fuels currently represents more than an investment in uranium and instead demonstrates a company developing its strategic role through various pieces. Uranium remains an important part of the investment case; however, it is no longer the sole component. White Mesa continues to evolve as a unique asset within the United States energy industry. Progress related to heavy rare earths is beginning to provide tangible support to a narrative that may have otherwise appeared too ambitious. Lastly, the ASM transaction is addressing an important gap within the value chain.

Am I suggesting the risk associated with Energy Fuels has diminished? I do not think so. As you can understand, this is a very volatile story, and while there is a great deal of work remaining in terms of executing the company's plans, I believe multiple items must fall into place. What does matter greatly to me is that I perceive a material difference from yesterday to today. Specifically, I am now able to look at Energy Fuels as having made concrete progress towards being what it potentially could become. And when comparing the abstract (narrative) and the concrete (actual progress), this makes it easier for me to consider a constructive view relative to my prior opinion.

For me, this is no longer simply a uranium story. It is a higher-risk but more credible critical materials platform, and that is why I am moving from "Hold" to "Buy".

From Uranium Name to Strategic Critical Materials Infrastructure

First, Energy Fuels is no longer merely a uranium price rally-exposed company, because in my view it is evolving into something much more complex, and increasingly difficult to categorize by traditional industry classifications. On one side, Energy Fuels is still the largest domestically based natural uranium-producing company in the United States with its assets located at Pinyon Plain and White Mesa, which remain central to the company's overall industrial operations. At the same time, the company is utilizing that very same operating footprint to create increasing exposure to a group of metals referred to as rare earths (including those classified as heavy) and is continuing its efforts to move further along the value chain through the ASM transaction.

In my view, White Mesa transitioned from simply being an asset that was valuable as a result of its ability to produce uranium to taking on the form of a multilateral U.S. strategic infrastructure. While Energy Fuels remains the owner of the only fully licensed and operating conventional uranium processing facility in the United States, it has evolved into the core component of the rare earths project. This is a very important material fact. An asset of such importance has a higher perceived value than could be assigned using history or typical financial analysis methods in terms of supply security and de-risking of industrial activities.

Of equal interest to me is another factor that changed over time. Although I thought that the rare earths segment had a compelling narrative potential in December, it was still largely untested. Today, in my opinion, the perspective on this issue must be revised. Since the production of high purity dysprosium and, more importantly, since the first kilogram of terbium oxide was produced at the White Mesa Mill, the story has taken on greater substance. While I don't believe this represents a completed transformation, I do think that the market now has sufficient additional data to begin viewing Energy Fuels not simply as a uranium company with some options available to it, but as a western-based platform in development for the critical materials supply chain.

A third factor that I perceive as different from previous times relates to this: uranium remains the closest economic driver for the Company, whereas rare earths offer a potentially transformative element that could alter the company's profile and valuation multiples in years to come. This is an important differentiation because it also helps explain why UUUU appears somewhat anomalous when examined through the lens of a pure play mining company. In reality, I perceive Energy Fuels as moving away from being viewed as a linear producer and toward being a company attempting to monetize multiple simultaneous industrial bottlenecks.

Earnings Season

I view the last reported financials by Energy Fuels through one lens: the trajectory of the company, rather than focusing on individual quarters. The historical EPS and Revenue Surprises Chart indicates that this is not a straight line, which is consistent with a non-linear accounting history. Specifically, FY 2025 had a net loss of approximately $86 MM ($0.38/share) vs. a net loss of approximately $47.8 MM (FY 2024), largely due to the additional expense associated with increasing the scope of the Group after acquiring Base Resources, increased SG&A and investment in exploration and development. Revenues were approximately $65.9 MM in FY 2025, representing a decrease of 15.6% year-over-year, but were greater than estimates of $61 MM.

In addition to these historical financial metrics, I think it would be a short-sighted approach to conclude here. The true message of the recent financial results does not indicate the income statement is "clean" yet. The true message is that Energy Fuels has shown tangible improvements in the underlying factors that will drive their financial performance from 2026-2028. In FY 2025, Energy Fuels exceeded its adjusted guidance for uranium mined, uranium processed, and uranium sold; specifically, they mined approximately 1.72 Million pounds, processed 1.015 Million pounds and sold approximately 650 Thousand pounds of uranium at an average sale price of $74.20 per pound. At this stage in the life cycle of a story such as Energy Fuels', I place more emphasis on the progress toward achieving goals than whether they exceed or fall short of analyst consensus for EPS in a single quarter.

I also see interest in how uranium's economics have evolved. Management again emphasized on the Q4 call that the production costs at Pinyon Plain currently sit in the $23-$30 per pound range and that the average cost of finished inventory sits at approximately $43/lb at the close of 2025. They expect the weighted average cost of finished inventory to decline from about $43/lb at year-end 2025 into the low-$30s during 2026, while expecting gross margins to reach 50%+ during this time frame. To me, this represents an important point because it suggests uranium could serve as the financial bridge to help fund some of the future growth expense for Energy Fuels. As I stated earlier, I do not believe it is simply a matter of producing more pounds. Rather, I believe Energy Fuels needs to produce those pounds at a cost that will enable the uranium segment to self-finance, at least in part, the expansion of the rest of the company.

Next Earning Season

There are three areas where I would focus my attention in the next quarterly report.

  1. First, the continued upward trend in uranium production. The continued use of both Pinyon Plain and La Sal to reduce average cost per pound and validate 2026 guidance will continue to show the uranium segment’s ability to create increasing margin and generate cash. As long as these two aspects of the business continue to grow in their contribution to profitability, the thesis will remain intact.
  2. Second, the transformation of rare earths from technical feasibility to commercial viability. Although headlines regarding the pilot program will not be sufficient evidence, I will look at the concrete details of Phase 1B & 1C of the project. In addition, I will look at the MREC (processing capacity) timeline for commercial production of Dy and Tb. Also, I will be evaluating indicators that confirm the White Mesa System has become an industrial platform, rather than a laboratory/research-type facility.
  3. Third, the vertical integration. Following the recent ASM announcement, the next quarterly report will provide the opportunity for the markets to assess whether or not the plan is viable as outlined. My primary interest in assessing the credibility of this aspect is not based upon when the acquisition closes. Rather, I want to have confidence in how White Mesa, the Korea Metal Plant and the future American Metal Plant will ultimately be integrated into a cohesive and functioning value chain.

Valuation

Clearly, my first point here is that the biggest issue with establishing a valuation for this company is clearly how we should establish valuation metrics. This company has a market capitalization of roughly $4.28B and enterprise value of roughly $3.42 B. With only these two pieces of information and using them to create ratios (multiples) for the purposes of comparison, based on the numbers (revenue of $65.9 M in 2025), one would likely conclude that those multiples are too high from a purely traditional standpoint. Bloomberg reports that the Current Enterprise Value/Sales multiple is 51.8x and the current Price/Sales multiple is 62.1x. It is difficult for most mining companies to explain multiples so high.

However, UUUU does not fit well into many traditional pure-play mining models. Using a short-term perspective, UUUU is obviously very expensive. However, if I view UUUU as a platform being built, and the uranium side is transitioning to a higher margin profile and the rare earth side begins to grow significantly in 2027-2028, the picture becomes much clearer. According to Bloomberg, UUUU should generate revenue of approximately $164.8 million in 2026, approximately $240.1 million in 2027 and approximately $356.3 million in 2028, while EBITDA will move from essentially breakeven or negative in 2026 to approximately $85.2 million in 2027 and greater than $110 million in 2028. As the multiples of EV/Sales decline to 20.8x in 2026 and to 14.2x in 2027, they begin to show some resemblance to what might be called reasonable, although it is clear they will always be high. Nonetheless, at least they can be seen as becoming somewhat interpretable if the market continues to reflect not just uranium pricing but also the strategic scarcity of the entire platform.

For me, that is precisely the issue: today, the market is not valuing a company that has arrived; it is valuing a trajectory. And that trajectory is quite aggressive. On the uranium front, UUUU is ramping up domestic uranium production. The company provided 2026 guidance that indicated strong year-over-year increases in production volumes and lower expected costs per pound produced. Additionally, UUUU has White Mesa, which alone places the company in a category distinct from virtually all of its U.S.-based competitors. Lastly, UUUU has the optionality associated with producing heavy rare earth elements ("HREE") along with the potential for a significant increase in downstream processing capabilities via ASM. Therefore, I do not believe it is productive to attempt to compare UUUU to companies that only mine and sell commodities. It is far too limited of a comparison given that UUUU is operating in multiple dimensions.

While it still requires investor discipline, I do believe that the market is recognizing the potential for UUUU to become an American-based uranium and critical mineral platform, which may allow for multiples higher than those of most mining companies.

One final statistic shows just how clearly the investment community now views UUUU. The estimated target price for UUUU according to Bloomberg consensus data is approximately $26.80, or about 48.5% above where the stock currently trades. Again, while I use analysts' projected targets as indicators of where the market is likely to go and do not use them as supporting arguments, they can show us whether even sell side analysts who know the many risks involved with investing in uranium also recognize the larger picture of the entire uranium space.

Therefore, while there are valid reasons to stay away from UUUU (i.e. unprofitability), I feel investors' view of value must adjust to reflect what management has done so far, the establishment of a multi legged American uranium/rare earth platform that deserves a premium over purely uranium miners.

3 Risks

1. Execution remains the key issue. Energy Fuels is trying to expand uranium production, White Mesa, advance ASM integration and develop several feedstock projects all at once. While i believe the strategy is compelling, the complexity alone creates the potential for an investor's entire thesis to be weakened by a single meaningful execution delay.

2. The stock's current valuation provides limited room for execution delays or other negative outcomes. Investors can no longer view the company simply as uranium trade; rather, they are pricing in future delivery over current financial performance. Any material delay in margin expansion, rare earth commercialization, or project completion could result in a significant reduction in the stock's multiples.

3. Timelines for both rare earth commercialization & projects are not yet fully de-risked. Although Energy Fuels has made positive strides with White Mesa heavy rare earth processing, the business is not yet a mature commercial earnings engine. In addition to rare earth, several other projects, including Donald, Vara Mada and the integration of ASM remain dependent upon timely receipt of all necessary permits, and successful operational follow-through. I continue to advocate a disciplined approach to investing until each of these components transitions from plan into cash flow.

Conclusion

I am still treating UUUU as a higher-risk satellite position rather than a core holding. My equity exposure is currently still about 2%-3% of my total holdings. Although the execution path remains difficult and the valuation is still not forgiving, the company has now made enough tangible progress for me to move from Hold to Buy.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: How Things Have Changed
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (XOM) has an integration both through geography and through vertical integration. But its partnerships also benefit others.\

Exxon Mobil and QatarEnergy announced the startup of the Golden Pass project, in which both are partners. The Golden Pass project is one of many LNG projects that will enable exports of LNG. In the case of this partnership, there is already underlying production pledged by the partners.

On the one hand, this points out that Qatar Energy will have some cash flow to help with repairs from the Iranian situation. On the other hand, Exxon Mobil has long been moving to an income and growth situation that will (most likely) offset any setbacks in the Middle East.

Many of these companies are self-insured to a certain extent and then reinsured after a certain amount of large losses. Probably the most famous reinsurer out there is Lloyds of London. But they are far from the only one.

Earnings - Last Article

The other thing is that when earnings for the fourth quarter were reported in January (and covered somewhat by the last article), the general forward look anticipated weaker commodity prices (especially oil) offset at least partially with stronger natural gas prices.

Now the Strait of Hormuz situation has largely sent the past predictions "out the window" because the current situation has sent prices soaring.

The last article noted that the Quant System on the Seeking Alpha website had this particular stock rated as a hold. Since then, the stock price has run up enough on the headlines to become rated by that same system as a strong buy.

For me, the situation is not quite that simple. First of all, the stock price has run up enough that this may be an opportunity to take some profits and wait for the inevitable post-conflict pullback.

On the other hand, there are many betting that the conflict would get worse because the Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed. That could well cause oil prices to go higher.

This "other hand" scenario has the benefit of the United States generally having a foreign policy that tends to leave things worse than we found them. Our long-term foreign policy strategy could use a serious upgrade. It is therefore hard to argue with this or the Quant System.

The last possibility is that the stock price has not changed that much. For me, this has been a long-term strong buy for some time (personally) because I believe that the growth-and-income strategy should more than offset any perceived possible pullback given the current stock price levels.

Therefore, while I would not argue with the views above because there are a wide variety of possible outcomes, I will likely go with the Quant System on the website and change the rating back to a Strong Buy because that has long been my personal option, even though I recognize a lot of possible winning strategies at this point.

First Quarter Earnings

There is now every possibility that first-quarter earnings will be giant due to the conflict in the Middle East. There could also be some conflict-related impairment charges due to the Middle East situation. Both of these have the possibility of continuing as long as the situation there continues.

The costs are already paid for in any given quarter. Therefore, the increase in the income from the higher revenue goes straight to the bottom line with only a few deductions (like taxes for example) because many costs are volume-based (not revenue-based). So, investors should expect that these latest prices in March for oil and gas could easily double net income for the month of March to more than offset the weaker commodity price beginning to the quarter. The exact effect will depend upon the timing of the sales and the sales contracts themselves.

This represents a material change of views from the fourth quarter. But then again, low visibility and volatility in this industry are pretty much normal. The reasons clearly vary. But when politics are involved, the investment environment can change quickly.

Back around the time of the last article and earnings release, I would have expected a continuing trend of weak commodity prices. Now, the SEC filing that happens before the end of the quarter will likely have some reference to the Iran situation and how long it has affected earnings.

To me, it seems as though the quarter started off with a continuation of weaker oil prices. But that is clearly not the case now.

Depending upon how the situation changes by the time the earnings conference call occurs, we may or may not have some concrete ideas about future quarters. Rarely is a material event as unpredictable as it is now.

Risks

The United States foreign policy is generally seen as needing a serious long-term upgrade. However, there is always the (small) chance that we know what we are doing long-term and come to a reasonable solution relatively quickly. Frankly, I cannot wait for one of these foreign policy decisions to actually demonstrate that it was well planned and well thought out. We badly need that improvement.

If by some chance a solution is reached such that oil prices stage a fast retreat back to where they were, that would likely result in a stock price pullback for traders to take advantage of.

However, I also believe that the growth and income strategy underway that features "advantaged products" discussed several times in past articles will likely produce higher stock prices in the future. I do not currently believe that the current stock price takes into account several years of potential earnings. Such a decision might encourage me to instead raise cash while getting back in later at a lower price.

Summary

Exxon Mobil is currently probably a net beneficiary of the situation in Iran. The whole situation is definitely changing the outlook for earnings in the current fiscal year. As the situation unfolds, we will know more about future earnings.

However, oil and gas has long been a low visibility situation. Therefore, unexpected changes often dramatically change the industry outlook.

The current situation is especially volatile and unpredictable because the average United States voter was never sold on the Iranian action before it started. Given the problems that may be caused by the "no sale" it may never happen again.

Now, once something longer-term is decided based upon the current "no sale" situation, then predicting some longer-term earnings could well be a far more realizable goal.

However, despite all the current challenges, for me this is a core holding and hence a long-term strong buy. The stock price has not really climbed enough (probably due to the lack of selling the public on this action before it started) to really worry about a cyclical peak.

If this conflict continues, per-share earnings of $15 to $20 or possibly even more are now a reasonable assumption. The final valuation will depend upon the market determination of how long the conflict will last. There are many possible short-term strategies that might work. For most investors, we could have a resolution to all those choices rather quickly due to what is known as the "War Powers Act" (even though it has a different formal title).

However, as a long-term buy-and-hold investor, the current situation is part of the "bargain" when one wants to hold onto an oil and gas company. As of right now, I am not worried about the possibility of a stock price pullback. In fact, I think the chances of a continued mess and hence higher commodity prices actually outweigh the chances of a quick resolution (and a retreat of commodity prices). But in this business, nothing would surprise me.

I would therefore hold this stock unless the price of the stock heads past $200 (because it might not see that price again until the next business cycle). Depending upon how the conflict unfolds and how high commodity prices go and their eventual duration, I could change my evaluation. But generally, I hold stocks until the long-term story changes unless an event like this causes a big enough price increase for me to realize several years of appreciation potential at one time.

data.title: Microchip: Growth Trend Improving After Major Plunge
data.description:
By Anthony Goh, Senior Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments

In our previous analysis of Microchip (MCHP), we highlighted the slowing growth performance of the company in 2024, which we believed was influenced by overall weakness in the DAO semiconductor market segment. We believed it to be a short-term headwind, while its long-term growth outlook remained positive, supported by its strength in MCUs and its wide analog product breadth.

We had expected FY2024 to be flattish based on prorated company results at that time and still low in FY2025, with recovery only in FY2026. But the company's revenues instead worsened in FY2024 and declined sharply in FY2025 with a 42% plunge. Based on its Q3 FY2026 YTD performance, its growth rate is improving (-0.9%), but still short of our previous growth expectations. At the same time, margins were below our forecasts as its net margins turned negative in the past 2 years, from 26.52% to -1.57% in TTM. Thus, in this analysis, we examine why Microchip’s recovery took longer than we expected and instead declined massively, especially in 2024, and whether it is now on track for growth and margin recovery.

Microchip Revenues Showing Positive Improvement After Steep Declines From PSP

In the first section, we examine the company's revenue growth performance trend to determine whether the company’s performance contracted much more than we had expected, as well as compared to competitors in the past two years during the DAO market downturn between FY2024 and FY2025.

Microchip’s revenue growth declined massively in FY2024 and FY2025, much worse compared to our forecasts, which we had assumed to be flattish amid the DAO market decline. Its revenue growth performance is also much lower than that of the DAO market. Thus, while the company may have been impacted by end-market weakness, it is not the only reason for the steep decline.

Based on management transcripts, we noticed that one of the main factors for the steep decline is related to its previous Preferred Supply Program ('PSP'). It was a supply agreement for customers that was introduced in Q1 2021 to lock in long-term orders and was non-cancellable.

By late 2021, PSP-related orders accounted for 50% of its total backlog and continued through 2022 and 2023. This led to a large surge in orders and revenue in the period. When the PSP was over, revenues slumped as customers had already stocked up on its products, and likely paused making more orders to work down their existing inventories. The only years where it had higher growth than the PSP period were in 2017 and 2019, but these were driven by acquisitions, including Atmel for about $3.56 billion and Microsemi for $10.15 billion in enterprise value.

Management highlighted that its customers’ inventory levels were in excess, which led to them cutting orders and contributing to the decline. This is evident based on the compiled table above of Microchip’s distributor customer days inventory ratio, which indicates the inventory levels of Microchip’s products at their distributor customers (distributors account for around 45% of total revenue).

As seen, its days inventory rose from 24 days in Q4 FY2024 to a peak of 43 days in Q1 FY2025, indicating inventory build-up at their customers. Positively, it has gradually improved over the period, though still higher than Q4 FY2023 levels.

Looking at one of its top distributors, Arrow Electronics (ARW) (accounts for 10% of its sales), its days inventory ratio has declined by 11% to 62.25 days in FY2025. The ratio was also improving each quarter in the past 4 quarters based on Gurufocus data, showing an improving trend that aligns with Microchip's commentary.

We thus believe the elevated distributor inventories were a key factor for Microchip's underperformance in the past two years, which we link to the effect of the PSP program. The good news is that it is now showing signs of normalizing, and we believe that the company’s distributor customer inventories could continue improving and support a positive outlook for the company, alongside the broader DAO semicon market segment growth recovery, which WSTS forecasts to grow by 7.3% in 2026.

Another positive sign that was highlighted by management was that its “book-to-bill ratio for the December quarter was well above 1, resulting in a much higher backlog entering the March quarter,” indicating that new orders were higher than shipments, and also gave strong YoY growth guidance for Q4 FY2026 at 29.8% YoY. We examine the DAO outlook and projections for the company in the next section.

Benefit From DAO Market Growth Recovery

In the next section, we examine whether it stands to capitalize on the DAO market recovery. We analyze the product segment growth performance and market outlook.

While the overall semiconductor market grew strongly in 2025 with 26.2% growth, the DAO submarket grew at a lower 4.8%, but still represented a recovery compared to 2024. WSTS forecasts also show the semiconductor market continuing to grow strongly in 2026 by 26.3%, led by logic (28.0%) and memory (39.4%), and, to a lesser extent, DAO (7.3%).

Although the DAO market growth is slower than logic and memory, it is still positive and accelerating, which bodes well for Microchip as one of the main players here. One of the main reasons why the logic and memory markets are growing more rapidly than DAO is their stronger exposure to the data center end markets. Logic chips like GPUs and CPUs are critical in data centers and memory chipmakers like Micron (MU), SK Hynix, and Samsung (SSNLF) are benefiting from the recovery in market pricing following actions to manage output growth in prior years, and HBM chips are critical for AI data centers too.

As we analyze Microchip’s performance across its three main product segments, we see that all three segments were previously affected by the sharp contractions in the FY2024 period. On a positive note, all three segments are now showing signs of an improving trend, with all three segments growing by double digits YoY in Q3. Based on management’s comments in its earnings call, the recovery appears broad-based across its end markets, which all had been in decline in FY2025. According to management in the Q3 earnings call:

We are seeing some recovery in our key end markets, automotive, industrial, communication, data center, aerospace and defense and consumer. They're all looking somewhat better. - Steve Sanghi, CEO

From a YTD perspective, Q3 FY2026 YTD performance for the MCU segment is still lower than the company’s historical growth (5.9%) as well as its Other segment (mainly FPGA products). One reason for the high historical CAGR for Microchip is the contribution from the acquisition of Microsemi in FY2019, when the segment grew 119% that year. Only the Analog segment’s Q3 FY2026 YTD growth is fairly in line with its historical and market forecast CAGR of 7.7% and 7.4%, respectively, as it returned to positive growth one quarter earlier than the other segments.

We expect Microchip’s growth recovery to continue, supported by improving trends across all of its segments and by the long-term growth of MCU, analog and FPGA product markets. In terms of its competitiveness in these product submarkets, Microchip continues to have strong product breadth. Based on our updated analysis, Microchip has the broadest MCU portfolio ahead of top competitors, as it focuses more on broader general-purpose MCUs compared to other players, which are more focused on the automotive MCU market.

In the analog market, Microchip ranks third in power management semiconductors, behind Analog Devices (ADI) and Texas Instruments (TXN) but ahead of peers such as STMicroelectronics (STM) and NXP (NXPI). For wireless and RF, Microchip ranks fifth, behind Analog Devices, Texas Instruments, NXP and STMicroelectronics. In the FPGA market, Microchip is third in product breadth, behind Intel (INTC) and AMD (AMD) (Xilinx), which are bigger players than Microchip as Intel and AMD acquired Altera and Xilinx as covered in our previous FPGA analyses.

We update our revenue projections for Microchip based on its segment breakdown. For our 2025 forecast, we prorate it based on its quarterly performance and incorporate management's guidance of 29.8% in Q4, which we apply equally across all three segments. For 2026, we assume growth based on the past two quarters’ YoY growth across all segments, as we expect the company to continue its recovery since distributor customer days inventory nears its FY2023 levels, as discussed in the previous section. From 2027 onwards, we base segment growth on the market CAGR forecasts from the table above. In total, we derive an overall growth rate of 5.91% in 2025 and 22.82% in 2026, which is in line with consensus estimates.

Profitability Improves As Growth Recovers

In the last section, we examine whether the company’s profitability could improve as revenue growth recovers. Microchip is a company with strong margins, with a 10-year average net margin of 12.04%, but it has turned negative to -1.57% in TTM from 24.98% in 2023 (FY2024).

As seen in the earnings and margins chart above, Microchip’s net margins in the past two years (2024 and TTM) were affected by declining gross margins, which fell from 65.44% in 2023 to 55.41% in TTM.

One of the main reasons is its IDM business model, where the company paused production amid elevated inventory levels, which affected its economies of scale. For example, in Q4 FY2025, management noted that “we continue to aggressively try to reduce inventory, so our utilization was low.” Management also stated that the company incurred underutilization charges, amounting to $156.9 million in Q3 YTD FY2026 and $119.2 million in FY2025, which corresponds to the weaker gross margins in CY2024 and TTM in the chart.

That said, management continues to emphasize its 65% gross margin target, which we believe is achievable as the company previously recorded 65.23% gross margin in 2021. We therefore expect Microchip’s gross margins to improve as revenue recovers and factory utilization increases over time.

The company also highlighted several initiatives to reach this target, including eliminating underutilization charges as production ramps up, restructuring fabs by closing its older Fab 2 facility and consolidating production into more efficient fabs, and shifting product mix toward higher-margin segments such as data center and aerospace & defense (which has increased as a % of revenue from 11% to 18% of revenue in FY2025).

Besides gross margins, other major cost components include SG&A and R&D, which were also affected as the company’s revenue plunged in 2024. R&D increased as a percentage of revenue from 14.37% in 2023 to 23.95% in TTM, while SG&A increased from 9.62% to 14.91% over the same period.

We overall model Microchip’s margins to improve gradually as we expect the company’s revenues to grow and its scale to improve. For our assumptions, we modelled the 2029 gross margins based on its 3-year average COGS % of revenue from 2021 to 2023, as we model its revenue in 2029 to recover to $7,121 million, which is above its 2021 revenue of $6,821 million when it reached a 65% gross margin. We then prorate our COGS % of revenue assumption for the years between 2025 and 2029.

We also apply a similar methodology for our R&D and SG&A assumptions, as we believe both were affected when the company’s revenues fell sharply over the past two years. Overall, we model net margins to converge closer to their long-term average, reaching 12.02% in 2026 and 21.72% by 2029.

Risk: High Debt Levels

One of the company’s weaknesses is its high leverage level. The company’s cash-to-debt ratio has remained weak for most of the past decade, declining from 1.0x in 2015 to 0.05x in TTM, which indicates that cash balances cover only a small portion of its outstanding debt. This was mainly due to the sharp increase in debt in 2018, when total debt rose to $10.3 bn from $3.1 bn in 2017, which we believe was largely driven by acquisitions.

Although the company has gradually reduced debt since then, from $10.3 bn in 2018 to $5.4 bn in TTM, its net debt remains elevated at $5.2 bn, reflecting that leverage is still a key weakness for the company. This may be a risk for the company to pursue more acquisitions to support a stronger growth outlook.

Valuation

For the valuation of Microchip, we update our DCF valuation with our revised revenue projections, which imply a 5-year forward average growth of 10.4% as we expect the company’s revenues to recover and grow broadly in line with the market. Based on a discount rate of 10.5% (the company’s WACC) and a terminal value based on the 5-year average EV/EBITDA multiple of major US chipmakers, our model indicates 31% upside from the current price.

Verdict

Overall, we believe the worst downturn for Microchip in recent history is likely to be over, as it has been showing signs of recovery following the steep declines in revenues in the past 2 years. We expect it to continue showing improvements in growth due to normalizing distributor customers’ inventory levels and a healthy book-to-bill ratio, combined with the overall improving trend in the broad DAO market, which we see Microchip capitalizing on and benefiting from due to its strong product breadth in MCUs, analog, and FPGA markets. With the improving growth momentum, we also model its margins to gradually recover as its scale improves. Compared to our previous DCF valuation, our updated model results in a lower price target of $85.15, which is mainly due to lower revised revenue projections accounting for the effects of the PSP program, though our price target still represents a Strong Buy rating as its stock price had fallen by 31% since the previous time we covered the company.

data.title: Ecopetrol: The Rerating Story Is Over
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

I have been exploring the energy sector lately and I am going to break down Ecopetrol S.A. (EC) in this piece.

Ecopetrol is an oil and gas producer. Basically, it refines crude into fuels, transports energy through pipelines and also operates power assets.

Interestingly, Ecopetrol checks the right boxes on paper.

It generates steady production and plans to pay a generous dividend of COP110 per share in FY 26. After a strong rally (currently close to its 52-week high), the stock still trades at roughly 11x forward earnings, which makes it look inexpensive at first glance.

However, this appeal fades once we examine numbers.

In Q4 FY 25, revenue and net profit dipped 18% and 61% YOY, respectively. Meanwhile, earnings also dropped 40% to COP9.03T for the full year.

Its profits are declining despite stable production. This points to an external pressure. Yes, the Brent crude oil prices were moving somewhere around the $70 range in FY 25, but the policy is hitting hard now.

To take this into perspective, taxes, including “conmoción” levies and a COP5.3T tax claim, are slashing earnings. Even more uncertainty is created by the CEO-related governance issues.

To be honest, all of this is hard to ignore.

Indeed, the dividend may look tempting, but it remains uncertain and tied to future payout decisions. In addition, cash flows are under pressure already.

Therefore, considering everything, I assign EC stock a Sell rating right now.

Stable Production but Profits Are Falling

From the outside, Ecopetrol seems to be doing exactly what it should do, but a quick assessment of its earnings completely flips the story.

Ecopetrol's revenue fell 18% to COP28.5T, and I would particularly attribute that to the lower oil prices. EBIDTA went down 16.2% to COP9.96T, but its margins clocked in at around 34.5%. It tells us that the cost structure remains tight, but then net income falls more than 60% to COP1.5T.

That decline can’t go unnoticed. Alongside, full-year profit is down almost 40% to COP9.03T, despite production remaining unchanged at around 745 kbpd.

This disconnect between profit and production is the whole point, and it raises a simple question: If operations are stable, what is pulling earnings down this hard?

Well, here’s the answer. New taxes, particularly the "conmoción interior" levy, and higher provisions have a direct impact on profits. On top of that, debt has climbed to COP109T, which means higher financial expenses. If we mentally remove these, pretax earnings appear more resilient. It tells us that this is more of a structural than an operational issue.

Hands down, this was a strong quarter operationally. Output was steady, domestic production was at a multiyear high, and refinery throughput set a new record of 430 kbpd. In a normal price environment, that would definitely have resulted in higher earnings. That said, international oil prices being cheaper than FY 24 and weak refining margins wiped out the benefit.

Looking forward, expectations are that EPS may barely budge. I think that is optimistic. It assumes stability in both oil prices and policy, neither of which has been stable.

That is the problem because the pressure on earnings looks like it would be permanent.

Politics, Taxes and Governance Are Weighing on Ecopetrol’s Profits

Ecopetrol is in a setting where politics and business go hand in hand. The more I think of it, the more it seems like policy affects, as well as drives, returns.

For example, take state ownership. This is not passive ownership. It comes with fiscal obligations. On one hand, the company has already paid COP0.6T in “conmoción interior” taxes in 2025.

On the other hand, the budget for 2026 requires approximately COP34.6T in dividends and royalties. What I see when I put the two side by side is the cash flow squeeze from both directions.

Ecopetrol has always paid high dividends, including special payouts in strong oil cycles, which illustrates how distributions are linked to the fiscal needs.

This is where the trade-off comes in. It reduces some flexibility and hampers the ability to reinvest. In simple words, it alters how you see long-term growth.

Then there is the tax burden, and this is where the numbers are striking. DIAN (tax authority in Colombia) issued a COP5.3T VAT claim on gasoline imports for FY 22-FY 24 at the start of this year.

It is significant enough to impact a single year's earnings profile compared to FY 25's net profit of COP9.03T. Although it is under court review, the imbalance is evident.

At the same time, governance risk complicates matters. Ecopetrol CEO Ricardo Roa, who was associated with President Petro's political campaign, has been charged with influence peddling related to a gasification project.

There are also broader allegations of financial irregularities. This is not merely a legal problem but also creates an operational distraction. The leadership attention is divided, internal alignment weakens, and the decision-making is less clear. Indeed, the associated board tension and union pressure only magnify the effects.

Finally, the credit backdrop brings it all together and illustrates how the market frames the company. The rating ranges from BB to BB+, depending on the agency, and is linked to Colombia's sovereign rating.

What this means is that company risk does not separate from country risk. Even with seemingly solid earnings, sentiment and valuation are very sensitive to political and regulatory developments.

Valuation Discussion

At around $15, the first thing to notice is how far the price has stretched away from its trend base. The 200-day moving average is at $10.27. It is not a slight gap but a complete disconnect. Even the 50-day at $12.96 shows the same thing. The trend has quickly moved up, and that sort of vertical repricing typically does not occur with strengthening fundamentals. It happens when sentiment and positioning get ahead of earnings.

That is exactly what the snapshot above shows. Earnings are not accelerating as forward EPS estimates sit around $1.32 for FY 26 and $1.35 for FY 27. Consensus revenue estimates are also stable to a little lower at around $32.4 billion in FY 26 to $31.5 billion in FY 27.

When I look at the bigger picture, there is no growth.

At around 11x forward earnings, the valuation does not look extreme on its own. However, it becomes a different story when the broader context is taken into account. This is a company with sovereign risk, significant fiscal dependency, and governance issues. All of these carry a discount to the market's interpretation of the multiple.

The market is not pricing it as a steady mid-cycle energy company. Rather, it is valuing it as a re-rated stock, but with a cap on the upside. That is why the multiple cannot continue to climb without an improvement in earnings.

Let’s be clear on how I see this situation.

My base view is that this move has already captured most of the “good news rerating.” The stock has shifted from undervalued to fairly valued at best.

More importantly, it has moved away from fundamentals. When this takes place, price is no longer driven by earnings upgrades but rather by sentiment swings and oil volatility.

That is why the risk is now more weighted to one side.

In a downside scenario, even if not a dramatic shock, a return to the 50- and 200-day averages is structurally expected. Those levels now represent where the last consolidation occurred. That suggests a drift back toward the $11-$13 zone where price previously found support.

In simple words, without acceleration in earnings, expansion on the multiple alone cannot take the stock so far.

Takeaway

The stock is saying one thing, but the fundamentals are saying something completely different. The re-rating has already happened, and what's left is a company with flat earnings but high expectations. There is no obvious driver to re-accelerate growth or reset sentiment.

If you are here for the dividend, you are taking on policy and tax risk more than business strength. For me, that doesn't warrant staying exposed. I’d be trimming into strength rather than adding here.

For now, the better approach is to avoid until we see that earnings can stabilize and fiscal pressures aren't getting worse. In the meantime, it's best to place capital in companies where growth, cash flow, and stability are all headed in the same direction.

data.title: QQQI And QQQ: The Ultimate AI Growth And Income Combo
data.description:
Overview

A little over a quarter ago I published an article titled: 'A Warning To Young Dividend Investors', which detailed why growth is important for investors with a long time horizon in the markets. As an investor that is bullish on the outlook of the markets and technology, my largest single position is the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). However, I still love collecting income from my portfolio because it adds some tangibility to my investments and adds a greater sense of peace of mind against financial uncertainty. As a result, I've paired QQQ with the NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), which mirrors exposure to the Nasdaq-100 Index (NDX) but also provides a high dividend yield of 14.1%. I previously covered QQQI and issued a buy rating, but I wanted to specifically revisit the fund to discuss how it pairs well with QQQ.

Although the indices have pulled back from their highs in recent months, we can see that both QQQ and QQQI have delivered attractive total returns over the last twelve months. QQQ's total return sits around 23.7%, while QQQI's total return trails behind at 20.8%. There are some clear differences between the two funds but I believe the combination provides investors with the opportunity to get the best of both worlds: growth and income. Furthermore, QQQI's emphasis on tax-efficient distributions adds flexibility for investors that want to limit their overall tax burden.

The Nasdaq-100 is basically the foundation for the advances made across the AI market and I believe that this combo is one of the most efficient ways to maintain exposure. QQQI allows investors to collect cash flow, even when equities are trending sideways or lower. For instance, markets have been selling off SaaS companies and forces traditional ETF investors to wait out the noise. However, QQQI is able to harness the volatility of the market to reward shareholders for their patience.

Of course, there are some clear tradeoffs that must be considered. QQQI will certainly underperform over a longer holding period and a period of sustained drawdowns can cause payouts to be cut. Even more importantly, there's always the risk that AI growth doesn't happen at the rate the market is anticipating. The sell off may continue if the large-cap leaders of the world continue to increase the amount of capital allocated to capex investments. So let's start by reviewing the strategy for both funds and how they work together.

Fund Strategy

QQQ and QQQI are nearly the same fund but they are wrapped in different packages. QQQ is the more traditional ETF that simply provides exposure to equities that make up the Nasdaq-100 Index. QQQ is a passively managed ETF so it has a very reasonable expense ratio of 0.18% of total assets. QQQ has total assets under management of $372B that are spread across 102 different positions. QQQ is rebalanced quarterly and reconstituted on an annual basis to ensure that holdings are always optimized.

As you would expect, QQQ is most heavily allocated to technology companies at 50.55% of assets. While QQQ is concentrated to technology, the risks are slightly offset by varying allocations to other sectors of the market. For instance, this is followed by an allocation to communication and consumer cyclical companies, which makes up 15.66% and 12.46% of assets respectively. I believe QQQ to be an efficient long-term position to buy-and-hold because it passively tracks the index and doesn't provide exposure to the small or mid-cap companies that can be seen as more speculative in nature.

Since QQQI also mimics the same index, the positions within are exactly the same. Therefore, investors are getting exposure to the highest quality businesses in the world. For instance, NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) is the largest holding at 8.64% of total assets. QQQI also provides exposure to other notable businesses, including Apple Inc. (AAPL), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), just to name a few. QQQI essentially makes it possible to collect income from companies that don't traditionally issue dividends out to shareholders.

In order to achieve this high level of income, QQQI actively writes call options against its positions. Since QQQI takes a more actively managed approach, the fund has a higher management fee of 0.68%. QQQI utilizes OTM (out-of-the-money) options to capture premiums and allow for some upside capital appreciation from its holdings. So if the index has a month where it climbs 3%, QQQI may be able to participate in this growth while also generating extra income from its sold calls.

QQQI is also uniquely capable of participating in moments of rapid growth due to its spread strategy. For instance, QQQI may write an OTM call with a strike price that is 3% above the current market price of the asset. Management will then take a small portion of the income received to buy a longer dated call even further OTM, which allows for even more upside growth. I believe that the idea here is for QQQI to capture the rapid jumps upwards during periods like earnings seasons where a stock can jump overnight due to a strong report. So unlike standard covered call funds that will cap all upside, QQQI is structurally aligned to a greater level of upside.

Despite QQQI's ability to capture upside growth, we must understand that there are still limitations. However, this is totally fine since the purpose of QQQI isn't to capture price growth. A good demonstration of this can be shown during the market's rebound last year, following the recovery from President Trump's tariff proposals. For instance, QQQ's share price increased 47.65% from the market's bottom through the end of 2025. Following the same time frame, QQQI's share price only increased by 26.7% for the same time frame.

Outlook

In my opinion, one of the main reasons to remain bullish on the outlook of technology comes down to the rapid transitions within the AI market. We are currently in the phase of massive capex spending so that businesses can build out their infrastructure to meet demand. This has been a big issue for the sentiment of the market as the large-cap leaders see their free cash flow levels plummet. Just for reference, here are the estimated CapEx spending levels for the leaders:

  • Amazon: $200 billion
  • Alphabet (GOOG): $175-$185 billion
  • Microsoft: $140 billion
  • Meta Platforms: $115-$135 billion

However, I believe that we will eventually get past these concerns and the market will start to realize the impact that AI can have on operational efficiency. AI can help businesses cut costs to levels that were once unimaginable. Businesses can reduce headcount bloat, improve the speed of internal processes, and operate products with much healthier margins. AI can help stimulate a productivity boom, through AI agents that can create autonomous systems capable of completing objectives without human intervention.

The outlook is extremely attractive and data suggests that growth will likely continue, despite short-term market noise like rising geopolitical tensions, interest rates, or rising unemployment. For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI market can eventually grow to a value of ~$3.5 trillion by 2033. This represents a potential CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 30.6% over the next few years. Even if this estimate is off by a 50% margin, this would still represent a potential double-digit upside growth of the AI market.

QQQ ensures that you own the companies building AI, while QQQI helps you capitalize on the market's skepticism by harnessing its volatility. When comparing their performance since inception, we can see how each fund plays its role. QQQ's total return sits around 38.1%, while QQQI closely trails behind at 35.89%. Looking forward over the next decade, I anticipate that QQQI will continue to underperform due to its limitations. However, this is fine because QQQI is only being used as an income generator that can protect capital and help during periods of uncertainty.

While I am optimistic about the outlook of technology, I think the road forward will be filled with a lot of challenges. Challenges will inevitability lead to volatility in the index, which is where QQQI can shine. Over the last six months, we've seen QQQ trade sideways due to the market's pullback. However, QQQI was able to harness this volatility and protect a slightly higher total return over the last six months. So while traditional QQQ holders are waiting for a recover, QQQI investors are getting paid now.

Dividend Utility

The irony of focusing on dividends is that I don't actually need to spend them yet. You may be asking, "why focus on QQQI then?", which is a fair question. However, I believe that the frequent income allows active investors like myself more flexibility to seek alpha. For instance, I collect roughly $3,800 a month in dividends, with some coming from QQQI. When markets are declining, I choose to let that capital build up and then strategically deploy it into opportunities that I determine to be undervalued.

This way, I always have the capital available to take advantage of declines so that I am never stuck waiting on the sidelines without the power to do anything. For instance, over the last few weeks I have been aggressively reinvesting all of my dividends into accumulating as many shares of Meta Platforms due to its attractive valuation. I call this the dividend wheel strategy and it is one of the many reasons why income generation within my portfolio is important. Conversely, this sort of income can be useful for retired investors so that they aren't forced to sell off positions during a market decline.

Since I believe that the technology sector will continue to expand earnings over the next few years, I believe it is better to utilize the dividend wheel approach, rather than reinvesting distributions back into accumulating more shares of QQQI. By redeploying QQQI's distributions into individual growth positions or QQQ, investors are able to directly participate in the growth of the sector. You are essentially locking in these growth positions at lower valuation multiples, which can help offset QQQI's underperformance in the future. So when the sector eventually rebounds, you are positioning yourself for attractive total returns.

One of the reasons why QQQI is an efficient holding alongside QQQ is due to the consistent payouts over time and its tax-efficiency. Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that there have been very few adjustments to its payouts. However, investors should always stay aware that payouts may shift over time since they are directly impacted by the success of the option writing strategy, the momentum of the index, and performance of its holdings.

Holding QQQI alongside QQQ can also have very little tax implications for buy-and-hold investors. This can be attributed to the fund's focus on return of capital distributions, which aren't classified as income and therefore aren't taxed as such. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 100% of the payouts issued YTD were classified as return of capital. Return of capital distributions reduce an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale.

However, long-term investors that have no intention to sell their position may not have to worry about tax-implications for a long time. It is technically possible to hold QQQI until your cost basis gets reduced down to $0, at which point all distributions would be classified as long-term capital gains, which are still tax-efficient.

Risk: NAV Erosion

QQQI is offering investors a double-digit distributions that is completely reliant on management's ability to capture sufficient option premiums on a continual basis. The reality is that it may be challenging for QQQI to always generate sufficient income during periods of prolonged market declines. Furthermore, the fund caps upside growth during market rallies, but there is no structure in place to limit the downside price movements. Therefore, QQQI will have a hard time recovering from rapid drops in the market.

When comparing the performance of QQQ and QQQI on a YTD basis from a pure price perspective, we can see how QQQI struggles to recover from the initial decline. The fund will still aim to payout distributions during periods where it may not have generated enough income to support those payouts. Therefore, these return of capital distributions are getting pulled directly from the NAV, resulting in NAV erosion.

If the outlook of AI shifts to a more negative sentiment and the downside continues, QQQI's share price can slide further. So it is totally possible to hold QQQI for several years and see very little price growth. It ultimately depends on if the Nasdaq's positive momentum can continue and we see a large drawdown at some point in the near-term. So I want to emphasize that investors must maintain realistic expectations and understand the purpose of pairing QQQI with QQQ. QQQI should only be a pure income play and a high yield of 14.1% doesn't necessarily mean that the fund will return 14.1% annually.

However, I want to be clear that a prolonged downturn in the technology markets is not my bear case. I believe that the underlying productivity gains and earnings growth potential remains strong, despite market challenges. In my opinion, the market tends to react dramatically in both directions.
